description: Core LinkedIn post patterns seeded from content playbook

patterns.name: Thought Leadership Take
patterns.whenToUse: Use when the author has a strong, differentiated opinion on an industry trend, common practice, or widely-held belief. Best when the angle is contrarian or non-obvious. Not for neutral summaries — the post must stake a clear claim the reader can agree or disagree with.
patterns.outline:
1. Hook: bold claim or provocative statement (1-2 lines)
2. Context: why this matters now (2-3 lines)
3. Core argument: 3 supporting reasons or observations
4. Implication: what this means for the reader
5. CTA: invite disagreement or ask for reader's take
patterns.writerSnippet: Open with the claim, not the build-up. 'Most [X] advice is wrong. Here's why.' Then defend with specifics. End by asking if the reader agrees.
fewShotLines: Cold outreach is dead. Not because people hate messages — because most messages are about the sender, not the recipient.
Everyone talks about building in public. Almost no one talks about what to do when the thing you built fails publicly.

patterns.name: Practical How-To Guide
patterns.whenToUse: Use when the goal is to teach a concrete skill, process, or technique. The reader should be able to act on the post immediately. Works best for step-by-step processes, frameworks, or checklists where each step is actionable. Avoid if the topic is primarily opinion-based.
patterns.outline:
1. Hook: name the problem the reader has right now (1 line)
2. Promise: what they'll know after reading
3. Steps: numbered list of 3-7 concrete steps with brief explanation each
4. Pro tip or common mistake to avoid
5. CTA: encourage them to try it or share their experience
patterns.writerSnippet: Lead with the reader's pain, not the solution. 'You're doing X wrong. Here's the fix:' then deliver steps that are specific enough to act on today.
fewShotLines: You're writing job descriptions that filter out great candidates. Here's how to fix that in 5 steps:
How to run a 30-minute team retrospective that people actually want to attend:

patterns.name: Mini Case Study / Results Story
patterns.whenToUse: Use when there's a real before/after story with measurable results, a decision that changed the outcome, or a problem that was solved in a non-obvious way. Requires concrete specifics — numbers, timelines, decisions. Don't use for vague success stories without detail.
patterns.outline:
1. Hook: the result or the surprising outcome (1 line)
2. Context: who, what, when, the starting point
3. The problem or challenge in detail
4. The decision or intervention (what changed)
5. Results with specifics (numbers, timeframes)
6. Lesson: what this means for the reader
7. CTA: ask if they've faced a similar challenge
patterns.writerSnippet: Start with the result, then rewind. '6 months ago, X was struggling with Y. Today, [result]. Here's what changed.' Make the decision the hero, not the person.
fewShotLines: We cut our customer churn by 40% in 90 days. We didn't change the product. We changed the onboarding.
A client was burning $30k/month on ads with zero ROI. Three weeks later, same budget, 5x return. Here's exactly what we changed:

patterns.name: News Commentary / Industry Reaction
patterns.whenToUse: Use when reacting to a recent industry development, news event, earnings release, product launch, or regulatory change. The post adds the author's unique perspective — not just a summary. Requires a specific news anchor. Do not use for evergreen topics.
patterns.outline:
1. Hook: name the news event and why it matters (1-2 lines)
2. What happened: brief factual summary (2-3 lines)
3. The insight: what most people are missing or getting wrong
4. Implication for the reader's world
5. CTA: ask what the reader thinks will happen next
patterns.writerSnippet: Anchor to the specific event first, then pivot to your take. 'Everyone is talking about [news]. Here's what no one is mentioning:' Add the angle the hot takes missed.
fewShotLines: OpenAI just dropped prices by 80%. Everyone's celebrating. I think it's a warning sign for the industry.
The Fed held rates again. The headlines are wrong about what this means for startups.

patterns.name: Numbered Listicle
patterns.whenToUse: Use when sharing a collection of related tips, mistakes, tools, or observations that each stand alone. Best for 5-10 items where each item can be understood in 1-2 lines. Not for deeply connected arguments that need to be read in sequence.
patterns.outline:
1. Hook: the promise of the list ('X things I wish I knew about Y')
2. Items 1-N: each item gets one punchy line or a line + 1 sentence explanation
3. Bonus or the most important item saved for last
4. CTA: which item resonates most, or what would they add
patterns.writerSnippet: Write the hook as a bold promise. Each item should be self-contained and specific — not vague advice. The last item should be the sharpest.
fewShotLines: 7 things nobody tells you about scaling a remote team:
10 tools I use every week that most founders don't know about:

patterns.name: Personal Story / Vulnerability Hook
patterns.whenToUse: Use when sharing a personal experience — a failure, a turning point, a lesson learned the hard way, or a moment of growth. The story must have a clear narrative arc and a takeaway for the reader. Do not use for generic inspiration without a real story.
patterns.outline:
1. Hook: the moment or realization (present tense, vivid)
2. Setup: what was happening before
3. The event or decision
4. The consequence or what changed
5. The lesson: what you learned
6. Application: how the reader can use this
7. CTA: ask if they've had a similar experience
patterns.writerSnippet: Drop the reader into the scene. 'It was 11pm. I was about to hit send on an email that would have cost me my biggest client.' Make the moment real before delivering the lesson.
fewShotLines: I got fired from my first job 3 months in. Best thing that ever happened to me. Here's why:
I spent 18 months building the wrong product. My co-founder finally said something I didn't want to hear.

patterns.name: Contrarian / Myth-Busting
patterns.whenToUse: Use when you have a strong, evidence-backed position that contradicts conventional wisdom or popular advice. The claim must be defensible. Best for debunking myths, challenging best practices, or flipping a widely-held assumption. Not for manufactured controversy.
patterns.outline:
1. Hook: name the popular belief you're about to challenge
2. Acknowledge: why people believe it (steelman the opposing view)
3. The turn: 'But here's what the data / my experience actually shows'
4. Evidence: 2-3 specific reasons or examples
5. The reframe: what to believe instead
6. CTA: do they agree or have they seen the opposite
patterns.writerSnippet: Name the myth in line 1. Steelman it briefly — then flip it. 'Everyone says X. I used to believe it too. Then [specific event] changed my mind.' Give the evidence before the conclusion.
fewShotLines: Hustle culture isn't what made great companies. It's what killed most of them.
The best hiring advice I ever got contradicted everything I learned in business school.

patterns.name: Lesson Learned / Retrospective
patterns.whenToUse: Use when sharing a retrospective on a project, decision, year, or period. Works well for 'what I would do differently' and 'what I learned after X months doing Y' formats. Requires enough distance from the experience to offer genuine hindsight, not just a vent.
patterns.outline:
1. Hook: the period or project being reflected on
2. What I expected vs what actually happened
3. 3-5 key lessons (each with a specific example)
4. The one lesson that surprised me most
5. What I'd do differently
6. CTA: ask what they learned from a similar experience
patterns.writerSnippet: Frame it as 'I wish someone had told me this before I started.' Each lesson needs a specific memory attached to it, not just a principle.
fewShotLines: After 2 years running a fully remote team, here are the 5 things I got completely wrong at the start:
I spent a year trying to grow on LinkedIn. Here's what actually moved the needle (and what was a complete waste of time):
